skill.name: create-pr
skill.description: Automates GitHub pull request preparation by reviewing branch changes, generating a PR title and body, pushing the current branch, and creating the PR with GitHub CLI.
skill.summary: Creates GitHub pull requests from branch diffs, commits, and QA-ready changes.
skill.author: Crearize

security_audit.summary: Static command findings are mostly Markdown code fences and inline examples, so the Ruby backtick and high entropy alerts are false positives. The skill does instruct agents to run git and gh commands that push branches and create pull requests, which is legitimate but state-changing automation. No prompt injection, credential exfiltration, malware behavior, or hidden network destination was found.

medium_findings.title: State-Changing GitHub Automation
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to run git push and gh pr create. This is expected for pull request automation, but it can publish branch contents or create repository artifacts if used on the wrong branch or repository.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines explicitly show git push and GitHub CLI pull request creation commands. The behavior is legitimate for the skill, but the commands modify remote GitHub state.

medium_findings.title: PR Body Shell Construction Requires Care
medium_findings.description: The PR creation example embeds generated PR body text through shell command substitution and a heredoc. The quoted heredoc reduces expansion risk, but malformed generated content could still cause command construction mistakes if copied without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command substitution pattern is present and used with generated PR body content. The heredoc delimiter is quoted, so this is a moderate operational risk rather than confirmed command injection.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static Ruby backtick execution alerts correspond to Markdown code fences and inline code examples. These are instructions for the host agent, not executable Ruby code inside the skill package.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious tokens appear inside Markdown formatting in SKILL.md. No Ruby file, script file, or automatic execution wrapper is present.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Documentation URL Is Benign
low_findings.description: The only hardcoded URL is a Claude Code attribution link in the generated PR template. No evidence found that the URL receives repository data, credentials, or user content.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 111 contains a static Markdown link to claude.com. There is no network request code or data submission tied to this URL.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto And Entropy Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alert points to natural-language metadata, and the entropy alert points to YAML front matter. No evidence found of cryptographic code, packed binary content, encoded payloads, or obfuscation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The inspected lines are front matter and a Japanese description. The file is readable Markdown and does not contain encrypted or binary content.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Push Command
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes a git push command that can publish the current local branch to origin.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command is explicitly shown as git push -u origin with the current branch placeholder. This is expected but state-changing.

dangerous_patterns.title: GitHub CLI Pull Request Creation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes gh pr create, which can create a pull request using generated title and body content.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command is directly present and is the core action of the skill. It requires GitHub CLI authentication and affects repository state.

content.user_title: Create GitHub Pull Requests from Changes
content.value_statement: Preparing pull requests by hand can miss important context from commits and diffs. This skill reviews branch changes, drafts a structured PR, pushes the branch, and opens it with GitHub CLI.
actual_capabilities: Checks the current branch before PR creation.
Uses git diff and git log to summarize branch changes.
Classifies backend, frontend, documentation, and other changes.
Generates a structured PR title and body.
Pushes the current branch to origin.
Creates and opens a pull request with GitHub CLI.
limitations: Requires GitHub CLI to be installed and authenticated.
Assumes qa-check has already passed before PR creation.
Does not resolve merge conflicts or failed pushes automatically.
Uses repository-specific paths such as backend, frontend, and documents.

use_cases.title: Prepare Feature Pull Requests
use_cases.description: Summarize feature branch changes and create a review-ready GitHub pull request.

use_cases.title: Standardize PR Descriptions
use_cases.description: Generate consistent sections for summary, tests, checklist, related issues, and screenshots.

use_cases.title: Speed Up Release Review
use_cases.description: Collect commit and file change context before asking reviewers to inspect a branch.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic PR
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner workflow after commits are ready.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a pull request for the current branch. Use main as the base branch and summarize the changed files.

prompt_templates.title: Create a PR for an Issue
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a pull request for this branch and link it to issue 123. Generate a concise title and include the test status.

prompt_templates.title: Review PR Content First
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze the branch changes and draft the PR title and body first. Wait for my approval before pushing or creating the PR.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Detailed Team PR
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workflow with structured release context.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a detailed pull request against develop. Group changes by backend, frontend, documentation, and other work. Include QA status, related issue numbers, and reviewer notes.

output_examples.input: Create a pull request for a feature branch that adds profile settings.
output_examples.output: A PR titled for the new profile settings feature, with sections for summary, backend changes, frontend changes, tests, checklist, related issue, and next steps.

output_examples.input: Create a bug fix PR after qa-check passes.
output_examples.output: A bug fix pull request with a focused description, changed file summary, validation notes, and a link to the created GitHub PR.

output_examples.input: Draft the PR body before publishing.
output_examples.output: A reviewable PR draft that explains the change, test status, checklist items, related issues, and any screenshots needed.

best_practices: Run qa-check and commit all changes before using the skill.
Review the generated title and body before allowing a push or PR creation.
Confirm the base branch and issue number match the team workflow.
anti_patterns: Do not run the skill from main or another protected branch.
Do not create a PR when local changes are uncommitted.
Do not accept generated PR text without checking sensitive details.

faq.question: Does this skill create the pull request automatically?
faq.answer: Yes. It can push the current branch and run GitHub CLI to create the pull request.

faq.question: What tools are required?
faq.answer: It requires git and GitHub CLI. GitHub CLI must be authenticated for the target repository.

faq.question: Does it run tests?
faq.answer: No. It assumes qa-check has already completed before the PR is created.

faq.question: Can I review the PR text first?
faq.answer: Yes. Ask the agent to draft the title and body before pushing or creating the PR.

faq.question: Does it work outside GitHub?
faq.answer: No. The workflow is designed for GitHub repositories that use GitHub CLI.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill runs commands that can push code and create GitHub PRs. These actions are legitimate but affect remote repository state.
